nodes.body: You swim the drowned moat, the current dragging at your rebreather and humming through the old submerged stone, and ahead, the enormous tide-gate arch rises out of the green dark. Its old wards gutter faint and cold against your approach, and the chill works into your wrists as you steady your line and kick on toward the gate. The sunken court waits below you like a single held breath.

You force a passage through the old sluice channel, silt clouding your lamp until the water is nothing but amber, and the narrow throat drags at your harness; the rebreather strains against the mounting pressure of the deep. When the murk finally thins, you rise beneath the great tide-gate arch. Its drowned wards gutter overhead, and the enormous court opens cold below you.

You climb the rival expedition's rotted scaffold, its old lashings slick and half-dissolved under your gloves. Every rung shifts alarmingly, but the structure holds long enough to carry you over the broken parapet. You drop down into the deeper, colder water, clear beside the towering tide-gate arch. The drowned royal court finally opens below you in the darkness and the patient cold.

The seaward culvert takes you fast, exactly as fast as it looked and exactly as wrong, and the current funnels you into a dead cistern with no air pocket and no way forward, the featureless walls closing green and blank on every side. You have no choice but to blow your reserve and surface at once. The dive is finished before it has truly begun.

Codename Sable, you reach the black water above the sunken royal court at slack tide, the one narrow hour when the sea holds its breath and the drowned halls lie still enough to enter. Across the reef the rival expedition's lights are already burning, so there is no margin left to waste on hesitation. Three ways lead down to the tide-gate: the drowned moat, the silted old sluice channel, and the rival's rotted scaffold. A fourth route, a seaward culvert, looks quick, and to your trained eye it looks badly, obviously wrong.

You set both hands to the rusted counterweight and wrench, and the corroded chain shears through in a sickening instant. The heavy gate slams down through the water and pins your harness hard against the sill, and for one long, freezing moment you are certain you will not come free again. You cut the strap loose at last and retreat to open water, empty-handed.

You trigger a burst of air to clear the choking silt, and the soft arch answers with a long, grinding shudder. Broken stone begins to fall in slow motion through the green water, sealing the passage behind you piece by settling piece, and you back out fast, kicking hard for the open sea. The only way into the court is closed for this dive.

The tide-gate hangs half-shut, its ancient wards humming a low warning through the black water; the counterweight chain is rusted almost through, and one hard pull could shear the whole gate down onto you. The silt-choked arch above is soft, so a careless burst of air might bring it collapsing. A flooded gallery mouth leads inward, a cramped maintenance crawl bores off to one side, and a warded march runs deeper into the dark. Choose your way in.

choices.label: Blast the silt with a burst of air.
Ease through the flooded gallery mouth.

nodes.body: Beyond the gate a submerged gallery opens wide, and the carved statues of drowned kings stare silently up at you through the green. Their stone faces are worn featureless by centuries of patient salt. Two archways flank the enormous hall, and a grand ceremonial stair spirals down toward the drowned throne level far below. Bubbles trail up from somewhere ahead of you, and unlit side passages fork away into the surging dark.

The left archway threads carefully past a toppled colonnade, the fallen columns lying like felled trees across the deep, undisturbed silt of the gallery floor. You pick your slow way over and around them, mindful of snagging your descent line, while the throne gallery begins to glimmer promisingly ahead through the murk. You hold your steady course and press on toward it.

You pry stubbornly at the barnacled reliquary door. It swings open onto a flooded stair that drops away without any visible end into cold and absolute blackness below. Your descent line snags on something, then abruptly parts, and for a heart-stopping moment you have nothing but the dark. You claw your way back to the gallery, shaken and turned around, the dive lost in the confusion.

The right archway bends past a caved-in refectory, its heavy roof long since fallen and settled into the surrounding silt. A shortcut sign in old court script points reassuringly downward, but a barnacled reliquary door stands much closer, tempting and ominously dark. You can trust the script and go down, pry that waiting door. Break off instead through the caved vault or the drowned gallery beyond.

choices.label: Pry the barnacled reliquary door here.

nodes.body: The grand ceremonial stair spirals down through drifting silt into the throne level, and each broad step is furred thick with a century of pale, undisturbed growth. You keep one hand near the wall and let your line pay out behind you. The great gallery opens below, vast and drowned and silent, and you press on down into it.

You start out across the cracked mosaic floor, and it gives way beneath you over a yawning black void, the ancient tiles dropping silently into nothing below. You kick clear at the last instant, but your lamp and your only spare line are gone into the dark. Blind now, disoriented and badly shaken, you feel your slow way back to the gallery mouth and abandon the crossing.

You lever hard at the sunken sarcophagus lids, and the old counterweights inside let go with a groan. A heavy portcullis drops through the water in answer, walling you off from the drowned circlet, and there is no lifting the thing and no way around it. You turn back into the cold with nothing to show for the risk.

The throne gallery yawns enormous before you, the great chair drowned to its carved stone arms. Above it the circlet of the last queen catches your lamp and holds the light; the mosaic floor is cracked over a black void, and the sunken sarcophagi look counterweighted and trapped, so both are baited traps for the greedy. A rival glow-stick already burns near the dais, and dark side passages branch off into the flooded court on every side. Work toward the dais, or gamble on a trap.

At the dais the rival diver reaches for the circlet in the same heartbeat you do, and the rival is measurably closer to it. Racing outright means trusting your speed against a diver with the shorter reach, a bad bet. You could feint low and steal the high approach instead, or cut the rival's tether and force a cold standoff here in the freezing water. The rival's free hand lifts, offering a sudden truce, and you cannot tell yet whether to believe it.

choices.label: Race the rival for the circlet.
Feint low and take the high approach.
The rival calls a false truce at the dais.

nodes.body: You race for the circlet outright, and the rival is faster than you, snatching the ancient regalia from beneath your closing fingers by a bare half-body-length. There is nothing left to chase; the prize is gone into the swirling murk ahead. Outpaced at the first piece, you fall back toward the gallery with cold hands and absolutely nothing to carry home for the trouble.

The deliberate feint works cleanly. You drop low, the rival lunges wide to cut off your imagined line, and the water between you and the drowned throne suddenly opens up wide and clear. You rise smoothly onto the dais in the gap you have engineered, the circlet abruptly within easy reach and the rival flailing uselessly behind you in the current. You press on to take it.

Your knife parts the rival's tether cleanly, and the current immediately takes the rival tumbling away into the green dark, arms wheeling in silent protest. The circlet is entirely yours for the taking now. You can seize it and go while the advantage holds, or stop to strip the drifting rival's valuable gear first. A heavy surge is already building through the gallery and every single second counts against you.

You turn back to strip the rival's abandoned gear, and those few greedy seconds end up costing you absolutely everything. A powerful surge slams through the gallery without any warning. The circlet slides off its ancient rest and vanishes into a flooded fissure, gone somewhere far beyond your reach or your line. You grope desperately after it and find only cold water, then retreat empty-handed into the dark.

The drowned circlet is your first regalia piece, cold in your hands and heavier than it should be, and its worked gold has gone grey and dull with the sea; you stow it carefully against your chest and take your bearings again. The gallery exit leads onward and down, toward the reliquary halls and the waiting second piece. You press on into the enveloping dark.

You pause to rest in a flooded alcove, letting your ragged breathing slow, and you pause a moment too long; the rising tide floods the only exit while you wait, sealing the passage with cold water and no air. You are cut off entirely, and you blow your reserve to reach the surface. The run is over, with a single piece to your name.

Past the throne the water darkens toward genuine black, and the way onward divides again before you. A level colonnade march runs straight toward the reliquary, and a low servant crawl bends down the very same direction through the settled silt. A flooded alcove offers a tempting rest, but the tide is rising fast and pausing here could seal you in for good. Side passages branch away into the surge.

You trip the ward-lattice with your bare hand. The ancient magic answers instantly with a concussive boom of pressure that ruptures the still water and your eardrums along with it. The entire world tilts sideways, all sound gone to a single high whine, and you cannot tell up from down anymore. Disoriented and half-deafened, you lose the reliquary and grope your blind way back the way you came.

You force yourself bodily into the flooded oubliette, and the narrow shaft constricts to nothing around you until you are wedged, unyielding stone pressing hard against your chest and your spine at once. You free yourself only by shedding your sample bag and the hard-won lead you had on the circlet, letting them sink away into the dark. You surface to regroup, lighter now and worse off.

The reliquary is a drowned vault of countless niches, and the tide-scepter floats tethered inside a trapped bubble of air behind a lattice of old wards; those wards are still live; touching them by hand would wake the whole vault against you. A flooded oubliette drops away to one side, narrow enough to wedge a careless diver for good. A rival marker buoy bobs at the ceiling, and side passages fork off into the surge. You can study the lattice, or chance it.

You hold perfectly still and study the ward-lattice, and its hidden rhythm resolves slowly out of the murk. It is the old court cadence, pulsing open and shut in a pattern you can very nearly count aloud. You can time your passage through the gaps between pulses, force the lattice apart with your pry-bar and hope it holds, or flood the trapped bubble to drop the scepter into your hands. Darker side passages wait as well, and the tide is turning.

You count the ancient cadence carefully and slide through a single ward-gap between two measured pulses, folding yourself small as the humming lattice sighs shut again behind you. The scepter tether is right there now, swaying gently in its precious bubble of trapped air. You close the last cold distance with great care and reach for it, pressing on to take the second regalia piece at last.

You set the pry-bar to the lattice and heave, and the overstrained bar snaps clean in your hands. The ancient wards discharge all at once, boiling the trapped bubble away into a roar of rising water, and the tide-scepter drops into a deep niche that seals over it like a closing eye. You are driven back into the dark with nothing to show.

You flood the trapped bubble deliberately, and the scepter drops neatly into your waiting hands as the ancient air rushes out around you. But the sudden inrush unbalances the entire vault, and heavy silt immediately begins to slough from the groaning ceiling above. You can take the scepter and run before it all comes down, or spend precious, dwindling seconds trying to shore the ceiling first. Side passages beckon uselessly around you.

You try to shore the groaning ceiling, but the wasted effort burns the thin margin you did not have to spare. The whole vault sloughs a slow avalanche of silt that pins your line and fouls both your fins in the dark. You abandon the scepter where it lies and cut yourself free, retreating to the cold gallery with the second piece left behind.

The tide-scepter is your second regalia piece, and its long ceremonial head is crusted thick with pale coral, grown around the old gold like a closing fist. You lash it securely beside the circlet and check your line once more. The way onward drops steeply toward the cistern vaults below, deeper and colder still. You press on down into it without hesitating.

Two separate ways lead down and out of the drowned reliquary before you now. A spillway shaft runs fast with channelled current, quick but wild enough to slam a careless diver into its rusted grilles. A slow silted stair descends the safer way, longer and thoroughly choked with a full century of drift. Side passages fork off into the surrounding dark, tempting and entirely blind, and the tide is turning against you.

The spillway shaft is genuinely quick, but the channelled current is wild and unforgiving, dragging you helplessly down its throat far faster than you can properly think. You can brace your whole body and ride the entire run out, trusting it to spit you clear at the very bottom. Catch a passing rung and slow yourself deliberately to a survivable pace. Darker detours branch off here too.

You ride it out to the end, and the spillway spits you straight into a rusted debris grille and pins you there mercilessly, the entire current holding you hard against the bars with a weight you cannot fight. You cut your fin harness free to slip the crushing pressure and blow immediately for the surface. Two regalia pieces are still waiting below, and this dive is over now.

You wade forward into the deep sump after the promised shortcut, and the silt beneath your fins has no bottom, swallowing your line length and then your descending weight. You sink helplessly past the point where your light finds anything solid. You drop your weights in a panic and rocket upward through the murk, aborting the entire vault run just to save yourself from the bottomless dark.

You set your chisel to the crusted vault seam and strike, and the ancient stone cracks along the wrong line beneath your hands. A cold flood-tunnel opens abruptly where you much did not want one, and the surging current pours through it and takes both your tools and your remaining patience along with it. You withdraw slowly into the vault, the seam a costly mistake, the shortcut lost.

The cistern vaults are a drowned cathedral of black water, ancient pillars marching away into a dark your lamp cannot reach the end of, and the queen's seal-ring rests in a drowned font at the far end of the hall. A deep sump offers a shortcut with no bottom to it, and the crusted seams could crack the wrong way and flood you. Rival bubbles trail up near the font, and side passages branch off into the pillars. Move for the font, or gamble on a shortcut.

choices.label: Wade the deep sump for the shortcut.
Move pillar to pillar toward the font.

nodes.body: At the font the rival is already there ahead of you, prying the seal-ring loose with quick, cold-numbed hands. You can confront the rival directly, though a struggle here would waste the breathing air of you both. You could shadow the rival and wait for a mistake, or bring the whole font arch down between you to trap the ring in reach of one diver alone; the stirred silt hides a deeper drop, and side crawls fork off into the dark.

choices.label: Confront the rival at the font.
The font silt hides a second, deeper drop.

nodes.body: You close in on the rival, and the confrontation becomes all silt and slow, freezing, dreamlike blows that land as though the whole cold ocean were between them. You can wrestle outright for possession of the ring, though grappling here will burn through the breath of you both alarmingly fast. You can knock the rival's lamp entirely loose and take the whole fight into the blind dark, where you know you move first.

choices.label: Kill the lamp and take it in the dark.

nodes.body: You wrestle grimly for the ring, and the struggle recklessly wastes the breathing air of you both in the cold, neither diver able to break the other's desperate grip. The rival tears free at the last and vanishes into the murk with the ring. Your own gauge is deep in the red now, and you must surface immediately with only two pieces and a hard, expensive lesson.

You hang back among the pillars and shadow the rival, silent and unmoving as the surrounding cold, and the long waiting finally pays off. The rival fumbles the ring loose, and it slips away into the silt in a rising grey cloud. You sweep smoothly in and close your hand on it before the cloud can settle, and you press on with the prize.

You bring the font arch deliberately down between you, and the heavy stone settles across the water with a resonant groan, sealing the rival out of reach entirely. The seal-ring is yours alone to take now. But the collapse has stirred the whole vault into slow, ominous motion, and a dropped rival cache glints temptingly in the silt nearby. You can snatch the ring and move, or greedily dig for the cache too.

choices.label: Search the rival dropped cache too.

nodes.body: You stop to dig hopefully at the dropped rival cache. It turns out to be a deliberate decoy, weighted stones wrapped in canvas and nothing of value. While you scrabble uselessly in the silt, the stirred ceiling settles further and floods the exit behind you with cold water. You barely surface, coming up with two pieces, empty pockets, and a hard lesson about greed.

The queen's seal-ring is the third and final piece, small and surprisingly heavy and worn perfectly smooth by a royal hand centuries dead. All three regalia are genuinely yours now, lashed securely against your chest. But the drowned court is visibly waking around you, the old wards murmuring and the water rising steadily toward the ruined observatory above. You press on and upward without delay.

With the full regalia lashed against your chest, the only remaining way up runs directly through the drowned observatory that crowns the entire court. A flooded orrery hall wheels its ancient brass slowly toward the ascent. A collapsed dome gallery offers a rougher climb in the very same direction. Side passages fork off into the rising dark, but the water is coming fast and there is precious little time remaining.

The orrery hall's great wheels of drowned brass turn slow and enormous in the current. Whole tarnished constellations of ancient metal grind against the shifting tide. You time your passage carefully between the moving arms, mindful of your line and your regalia. The observatory hub opens beyond them at last, and you press on toward the distant light above.

You trust the visibly cracked dome ribs with your weight, and one of them fails suddenly beneath your hand; a heavy slab swings down and pins you against the gallery floor in the freezing cold. You cut yourself free in a desperate scramble, but the regalia bag tears open in the struggle. The seal-ring is lost to the dark below, and you surface with only two pieces.

The collapsed dome gallery is a treacherous maze of leaning slabs. Its ancient ribs are visibly cracked and untrustworthy where they still arch overhead. A low crawl runs beneath the wreckage toward the observatory, and a high ledge angles the same way directly over the cracked ribs. The high road is faster and far more likely to come crashing down on you, and side crawls fork off into darkness.

The observatory antechamber is a vast drowned clock of brass rings, enormous and utterly still, and high above it the ascent shaft's light hangs like a single distant coin on the black water. You are genuinely close now, and so, unfortunately, is the rival. You fix that faraway coin of light firmly in your mind and press on toward the control ring.

You thread carefully through the great brass rings of the drowned clockwork, ducking through the narrow gaps where the ancient mechanism has seized and stopped forever. The rival's lamp swings and wheels somewhere ahead of you in the murk, closer now than it was before. You keep your descent line well clear of the sharp metal and press on after it.

A drowned catwalk crosses the antechamber directly ahead. Its ancient metal groans under the shifting weight of the incoming tide. You test each span carefully before you trust it, the full regalia heavy against your chest and slowing you. The rival's lamp swings somewhere beyond, and you press on across the groaning span.

Past the observatory threshold the water thins to a cold breath of trapped air, and it is the first air you have tasted since the surface, stale and sharply metallic in your throat. The rival is somewhere ahead of you in the murk, moving fast for the control ring. You cannot let that lead keep growing, so you press on hard through the cold.

You throw yourself at the seized shutter mechanism, forcing it against a century of corrosion, and the machine fights back. A heavy counterweight drops across the only stair with a booming crash of settling stone, sealing you below the observatory. You are well and trapped now. You must ration your air back to the surface with the regalia but no way to seal the court.

The drowned observatory crowns the entire court, its shattered dome open to a distant, wavering shaft of surface light far above your head. Here the old tide-mechanism could reseal the whole court behind you, but the machine is seized with corrosion and forcing it could drop the stair on your head. The rival has already beaten you to the control ring, and the water is still rising around you. Side passages and flooded side-corridors branch off into the dark. Move on the rival, or force the shutter.

choices.label: Move on the rival at the control ring.

nodes.body: The rival holds the observatory control ring, the very last obstacle between you and a clean ascent with the full regalia. You can bargain openly for a shared surfacing, trading one piece for a clear climb, or try to out-climb the rival to the shaft. You could race the rising water straight up and abandon the mechanism entirely. The rival feints toward the ascent shaft, and above you a whole wing of the shattered dome shifts and peels loose. Choose now, before the water chooses for you.

choices.label: Out-climb the rival to the shaft.
Race the rising water straight up.
The rival feints toward the ascent shaft.
A wing of the dome peels loose above the control ring.

nodes.body: The rival will trade a clear, unopposed ascent for a single regalia piece, hands held open in the cold water to demonstrate a fragile good faith. You can give up the drowned circlet and keep the scepter and the ring, hand over the seal-ring instead, or refuse the whole bargain outright and gamble everything on beating the rising water to the shaft. That gamble could easily drown you both. Side passages wait uselessly.

choices.label: Refuse and gamble on the water.

nodes.body: You refuse the offered bargain and gamble instead on the water, and the desperate gamble fails you. The rising tide beats you both to the shaft. The rival, lighter and unburdened by regalia, escapes up it just ahead of you. You claw your exhausted way up last, empty-handed and outpaced, breaking the surface with nothing but the cold and the bitter story of it.

You out-climb the rival directly up the drowned observatory ribs. You haul yourself hand over hand, faster than the rising water and faster than the rival below you. You reach the control ring with the full regalia still slung against your chest. The ancient mechanism is finally within reach, and you press on toward it, breathing hard in the thin air.

Racing the rising water, you reach the very foot of the ascent shaft a single stroke ahead of the rival. The tide-mechanism is now behind you rather than before you. You can turn back to seal the drowned court and lose those hard-won seconds, or climb immediately and leave the court open to the sea forever. Side passages tug insistently at the ragged edge of your failing light.

choices.label: Turn back to seal the court.
Climb now, leave the court open.

nodes.body: At the control ring, with all three regalia lashed against your chest, you can finally work the old tide-mechanism to reseal the drowned court behind you for good. You can seal it fast and rough, trusting a half-set catch to hold against the tide. You can seal it slow and true, at the real cost of air you can barely spare. Or you can skip the seal entirely and guarantee your own clean ascent, leaving the court open to the patient sea.

choices.label: Skip the seal, secure your ascent.

nodes.body: You throw the ancient mechanism fast, and the seal does catch, but the corroded old works groan loudly and hang there half-set, neither properly open nor fully shut. You can force it the rest of the way home and spend the precious air you do not have to spare, driving the stubborn catch home. Accept the partial seal for exactly what it is and start your long climb now while you still can.

choices.label: Accept the partial seal and climb.

nodes.body: You force the stubborn mechanism the last of the way fully home, and the drowned court groans shut around you, the old wards flaring one final time in the black water before they go dark for good. The ascent shaft stands clear and open above you now, that distant coin of surface light patiently waiting. You press on upward toward it at last.

The slow, true seal demands considerably more patience than you have left in you. You give it anyway, working the ancient mechanism carefully through its entire cadence. The whole court locks clean behind you, regalia fully recovered and every ward accounted for and finally quiet. The ascent shaft waits above you, and you press on up into the climbing, decompressing cold.

The shaft climbs slowly through cold decompression stops toward the far coin of surface light, and the rival is climbing the same cold water somewhere below you. Here at the end your own labouring blood becomes the real danger. You can hold the stops properly, hovering out the long, aching minutes your body demands of you. Or you can push the ascent hard to beat the rival up, gambling that your blood forgives you the reckless shortcut this one last time.

choices.label: Push the ascent to beat the rival.

nodes.body: You push the decompression stops, climbing considerably faster than your blood should ever allow, the dissolved nitrogen already singing a warning through your aching joints. This is a genuine gamble against your own body, and it is not quite too late to fold the hand. You can ease off deliberately at the last stop and let your blood settle. Commit fully to the fast surface and take whatever it costs.

choices.label: Ease off at the last stop.

nodes.body: You break the surface into open daylight with the full regalia lashed against your chest. Far behind and below you the drowned court is sealed perfectly clean, the rival left down in the dark to surface empty-handed. You held the very last stop, and your blood forgives you the dive entirely. The recovery is genuinely complete, the circlet and scepter and ring all recovered and the sunken court left in peace to keep its ancient dead. It is as good a dive as you have ever made, and you know it in your bones.

ending.title: The Court Sealed, The Regalia Home

nodes.body: You surface at last with the full regalia and a good, clean seal on the court behind you, having traded only a single skipped stop and a splitting headache for the margin you needed. Your ears ring and your joints ache badly with the fast last stretch, but nothing worse than that follows you up into the light. The court is closed and quiet below you now, and all three pieces ride securely home against your chest. A genuinely good day's work, paid for in a small enough coin.

ending.title: All Three, and the Court Closed

nodes.body: You make the surface fast and rough, the full regalia entirely intact and the court sealed clean behind you, but your labouring blood does not forgive the reckless shortcut. The bends set their teeth deep in your joints, and you will spend a genuinely hard week ashore paying in full for these last few minutes. Still, it is a clear win, whole and undeniable: all three pieces home and a drowned court finally closed. A hard victory, and one you have paid for honestly.

With the drowned court only half-sealed below you, you climb the shaft steadily through the cold decompression stops, the regalia whole against your chest but the old wards still murmuring faintly somewhere in the deep. It is a job mostly done, not quite perfectly done. You hold your stops properly and press on up toward the waiting surface light above.

You surface at last with all three regalia pieces lashed against your chest. The drowned court stands only half-sealed below, its old wards still murmuring restlessly in the dark. The recovery itself is genuinely complete and undeniable, the circlet and scepter and ring all safely home against you. The full sealing will simply need a second dive on another slack tide, when the winter sea lies still enough to enter. It is a clever partial win, and you will gladly take it and come back for the rest.

ending.title: The Regalia Up, The Court Ajar

nodes.body: Leaving the drowned court open behind you for the tide to keep, you strike hard for the ascent shaft with the full regalia slung against your chest. A rival trailing line hangs ready and convenient right beside your own, honestly too convenient by half. You can climb your own line, slow and entirely certain, or trust the rival's waiting rope instead and save yourself the precious time you no longer have.

You take the rival's trailing line, and it is a deliberate trap, weighted and rigged to foul. It drags you down the moment you trust your weight to it, and you cut free just before it drowns you. But the regalia bag tears loose in the tangle and sinks away into the dark, and you surface with nothing but the cold and the story of it.

You surface at last with the full regalia lashed against your chest, and the drowned court is left open behind you now, for the sea and the tide to keep as they please. All three pieces are recovered, whole and finally home, but the sealing is refused and the court will stay restless and wakeful below. It is a complete victory, and a decidedly colder one than it might have been. It is undeniably yours, and you carry it up into the light.

ending.title: Regalia Recovered, Court Left to the Sea

nodes.body: You hand the drowned circlet carefully across to the rival, and the uneasy trade holds together. Under a watchful, mutually suspicious truce you both climb steadily for the ascent shaft, the scepter and the seal-ring still yours against your chest. You keep one wary eye fixed on the rival for the entire ascent and press on upward through the decompressing cold.

You surface at last with the tide-scepter and the seal-ring lashed against your chest. The drowned circlet is gone to the rival, handed over in exchange for a clear ascent. Two of the three regalia are recovered, and a rival is now owed a favour that may well come due someday. It is a clever partial haul, bought entirely without blood, and you gladly take the two you have and count the whole day a good one.

ending.title: Two of Three, a Truce Kept

nodes.body: You give up the queen's seal-ring and take the clear ascent the rival openly offers, the circlet and the scepter held secure against your chest. The fragile truce holds together just long enough to matter, and you climb steadily for the shaft without a blade waiting at your back. You press on upward toward the distant surface light.

You surface at last with the circlet and the tide-scepter lashed against your chest, and the queen's seal-ring is traded honestly away to buy your clean climb up the shaft. Two regalia pieces come home whole and undamaged, and the drowned court keeps its very last piece and guards it still below. It is a measured partial win, taken entirely without a fight. You have learned the true shape of the court, and you will come back for the ring.

ending.title: The Ring Given, Two Brought Home

nodes.body: You probe the winch-chain gallery, and the heavy old chains come suddenly alive in the current, swinging blind and dangerous through the murk all around you. You back out fast before one of them can catch you, but your careful bearings go swinging out with them. The rattling gallery turns you back into the cold, the entire run set back to another dive.

You follow the eel-run drain deeper, and it narrows and twists relentlessly until it is barely wider than your own shoulders, leading nowhere useful at all. There is no room whatsoever to turn around, only to reverse blind through the silt. You back the entire way out, and the twisting drain turns you back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You test the maintenance crawl carefully, and it chokes down to nothing in the dark, a dead throat of packed silt and fallen stone. There is no way through it and no reward at its end, only the cold and your steadily wasted air. You reverse out along your line, the crawl a dead end, the run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its very end, and it pinches shut in the silt, the walls closing steadily to a seam too narrow for any diver alive to pass. You lose your careful bearings entirely in the turning-around, the murk all the same in every direction. You give up the dead branch and fall back, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, splitting further the deeper you let your light probe into it. A winch-chain gallery rattles ominously somewhere off to your left, an eel-run drain gapes darkly to your right, and a low maintenance crawl bores straight ahead into the murk. You can probe any one of the three, or push the whole branch to its end and see where it lets out.

choices.label: Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: You stop to read the drowned kings' inscriptions, tracing the worn old script carefully with your light, but the long-dead kings say nothing you can actually use. The letters dissolve entirely into salt-blur and meaningless court cant. The reading costs you precious air and gains you absolutely nothing, and you turn back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You pocket the loose gilt tessera, and it is a genuine fool's trinket, a mere fleck of gilded paste worth precisely nothing at all. Worse still, prising it free shifts the whole wall, and heavy silt pours suddenly down to blind you. You back carefully away from the mess you have made and turn back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its distant end, and it pinches shut in the silt, the walls narrowing steadily to a seam no wider than your own clenched fist. Turning around in the tight choke, you lose all sense of the way back entirely. You grope your slow way out at last and give up the branch, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, opening onto the ancient gallery of the kings. Worn drowned inscriptions cover one entire wall, and a single loose gilt tessera winks in your lamp, tempting and almost certainly worthless. You can stop to read the dead kings' inscriptions, pocket the small trinket. Push the passage onward to its end into the deeper, waiting black.

choices.label: Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: You search the empty courtier galleries thoroughly, drifting past niche after looted niche, and find nothing whatsoever but old dust long since settled into silt. Whatever was once here is entirely centuries gone. The galleries give you only cold and spent minutes, and you turn back the way you came, the run set back to another dive.

You dredge the drowned offering pool, sifting the black silt patiently for treasure, and come up with only fistfuls of corroded, worthless nothing. The pool has been picked entirely clean by tide and by others long, long before you arrived. The dredging wastes your air, and you turn back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You force the carved choir-screen, and it gives way with a grinding crack onto a flooded dead space with nothing behind it but more cold dark. The whole wasted effort has cost you badly and gained you a single empty, echoing room. You turn back through the broken screen into the cold, the entire run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its end, and it pinches shut in the silt, the walls closing steadily to a seam far too tight for any diver to pass. You lose your bearings completely in the narrow turning, the dark identical on every single side. You fall back along your line and give up the branch, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, branching away around the throne level. Empty courtier galleries run off one way, an offering pool glimmers faintly another, and a carved choir-screen blocks a third route entirely. You can search the looted galleries, dredge the drowned pool, force the carved screen. Simply push the passage to its end and be done with the whole detour.

choices.label: Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: You detour through the drowned kitchens, past ranks of fallen copper and a great cold hearth, and the whole low ceiling is furred thick with silt that lifts and billows at your slightest passing. The rising murk blinds you entirely. You feel your slow way back out of the kitchens into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You climb the rotten minstrel gallery, its crumbling rail dissolving to nothing under your gloves, and the floor above suddenly gives way in a slow slump of falling stone. You kick clear of the collapse just in time, badly shaken and thoroughly turned around. The gallery turns you back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its distant end, and it pinches shut in the silt, closing steadily to a seam a living diver simply cannot pass. In the tight turning-around you lose the whole thread of the way back entirely. You grope your way out along your line and give up the branch, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, past the throne's outer ceremonial halls. The drowned kitchens open low and greasy to one side, and a rotten minstrel gallery climbs into the murk on the other. You can detour through the fallen kitchens, climb the crumbling minstrel gallery, or simply push the passage onward to its end into the cold and the silt.

choices.label: Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: You rifle the reliquary's lesser niches, working fast and methodically along the wall, but they hold only broken reliquary and salt-eaten worthless scraps. Nothing here at all is worth the precious air you are steadily spending on it. You give up the empty niches and turn back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You chase the rival marker buoy through the murk, thinking it must mark some real prize, and it leads you only in a slow, useless circle back to nowhere. The rival set it there precisely to waste your particular kind of curiosity. The chase burns your margin, and you turn back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You pry stubbornly at the sealed ambry, entirely certain it hides something worth the effort, and it opens at last onto a flooded void, swept perfectly bare a full century ago. There is nothing inside it but cold water. The prising has cost you dearly, and you turn back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its end, and it pinches shut in the silt, the walls narrowing steadily past any hope whatsoever of passage. You lose your bearings completely in the tight choke, every direction the exact same black murk. You fall back along your line and give up the branch, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, threading through the reliquary's lesser vaults. Minor niches line one entire wall, a rival marker buoy drifts tauntingly ahead, and a sealed ambry waits shut and stubborn at the end. You can rifle the lesser niches, chase the rival buoy, pry the sealed ambry, or simply push the passage to its distant end.

choices.label: Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: You take the flooded scriptorium turn, and the drowned shelves have collapsed entirely across the passage. It is a thick slurry of rotten vellum and fallen stone, and there is simply no way through the ruin of it now. You reverse carefully out of the scriptorium and back into the cold, the whole side-branch a dead loss, the run set back to another dive.

You follow the rope of bubbles up, entirely certain it promises real air, and it delivers you only to a sealed pocket against unbroken stone, a false ascent with no possible surface above it at all. The bubbles were a lie the court told you. You sink slowly back down into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its distant end, and it pinches shut in the silt, closing to a narrow seam no wider than a single hand; turning in the tight choke you lose all sense of the way you came. You grope your way out along the line and give up the branch, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, running below the reliquary halls. A flooded scriptorium turns off to one side. A rope of silver bubbles rises tempting toward some false promise of air somewhere above you. You can take the flooded scriptorium turn, follow the treacherous bubbles up, or simply push the passage onward to its end into the black.

choices.label: Follow the rope of bubbles up.
Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: You sound the pillar-forest carefully for a shortcut, tapping pillar to pillar into the dark, and the endless ranks of stone go on identical far past the reach of your light. You lose your count and your careful bearings among them entirely. The forest turns you back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You drain-dive the overflow weir, letting the strong current draw you down into its dark throat, and it very nearly does not let you back out again. You fight the sucking pull free by desperate inches, hard used and thoroughly turned around. The weir drives you back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You search the drowned wellhouse, squeezing awkwardly into its cramped stone shell, and find only a dead shaft dropping into lightless water with nothing whatsoever at its distant bottom. There is no reward here at all and no way onward. You back out of the wellhouse into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its end, and it pinches shut in the silt, the walls closing steadily to a seam far too tight to force a passage. You lose your bearings entirely in the narrow dark, turned around and utterly blind. You fall back along your line and give up the branch, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, winding among the cistern pillars. The pillar-forest promises some shortcut hidden somewhere in its endless ranks, an overflow weir drains off darkly to one side, and a drowned wellhouse squats shut at the end. You can sound the pillars, drain-dive the weir, search the wellhouse, or simply push on to the end.

choices.label: Sound the pillar-forest for a shortcut.
Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: You cut through the sunken observatory stores, and the heavy shelves have all come down across the passage. It is a landslide of drowned crates and fallen stone, and the entire way is impassably choked now. There is nothing to salvage here and nowhere left to go. You reverse carefully out of the ruined stores and back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You try the astronomer's stair, and it climbs only into a collapsed observatory cell; the little room is sealed over with fallen dome and dead-ended in the absolute dark, and there is nowhere further up to go from here. The climb has cost you air for nothing. You descend the useless stair again into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its distant end, and it pinches shut in the silt, narrowing to a seam a living diver simply cannot pass; you lose the whole thread of the way back in the tight, blind turning. You grope out along your line and give up the branch, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, running below the ruined observatory. The sunken observatory stores open low and cramped to one side, and an astronomer's stair spirals up into the murk on the other. You can cut through the collapsed stores, try the astronomer's stair. Simply push the passage onward to its end into the cold and the settled silt.

choices.label: Cut through the sunken observatory stores.
Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: You salvage at the shattered dome instruments, prising loose a drowned brass astrolabe, and the entire ruined rack shifts and slides suddenly toward you. You let the salvage go at once and kick hard clear of the slow avalanche of metal. The instruments turn you back into the cold, the run set back to another dive.

You signal the surface far too early, firing a marker up the shaft before the regalia is anywhere near secure, and the premature flare only draws the rival's sharp attention to your exact position. You have given away your whole hand for nothing at all. You turn back into the cold to salvage what you can, the run set back to another dive.

You push the side passage to its end, and it pinches shut in the silt, closing steadily to a seam no diver alive could ever pass. You lose your bearings entirely in the tight choke, the murk exactly the same on every single side. You fall back along your line and give up the branch, the run set back to another dive.

A side passage forks off the main route into the drowned dark, threading the observatory's shattered ruin. Broken dome instruments lie scattered and temptingly salvageable to one side, worth a fortune on the surface if you could only carry them. A surface signal beckons you toward an early, foolish ascent. You can salvage the drowned instruments, signal the surface far too early, or simply push the passage on to its end.

choices.label: Push the passage to its end.

nodes.body: The clever feint leads you straight into a dead gallery. The rising water seals its only mouth behind you before you even understand your costly mistake. You double desperately back through the cold and the murk, but the crucial seconds are already gone, and so is the rival. You surface late and entirely empty, cleanly outplayed at the very last moment.

You commit fully to the rival's feint toward the ascent shaft, chasing the fleeing shadow up and away from the control ring. The dark closes in steadily around you as you go, and the rising water hisses somewhere alarmingly close. You follow the sudden movement hard, and you press on after it.

The loose dome drives you bodily off the control ring in a grinding slide of stone, and the narrow seal window closes shut along with it. You are forced up and away with the full regalia still against your chest, but the court left standing open behind you, the mechanism now entirely out of reach for this dive. You climb for the surface.

A whole wing of the shattered dome peels loose above the control ring, and you commit to riding out the avalanche of falling stone rather than fleeing it now. The dark closes in around you in a churning cloud of silt and settling rubble from above. You brace yourself hard against the ring and press grimly on through the chaos.

The deeper drop swallows your descent line whole, paying out to its very last meter and then hanging slack and useless in the black water below you. There is no bottom you can possibly reach down here and no ring waiting after all. You haul yourself out with nothing, the seal-ring still somewhere above and the dive set back to another day entirely.

You follow the disturbed font silt downward, entirely certain it hides some shortcut, and it does hide something after all: a second, deeper drop yawning wide open beneath the first. Your descent line pays steadily out into it, and the dark closes in around you as you go. You commit fully to the drop and press on down.

The offered truce is a deliberate stall, and you fall for it completely. While you wait for terms that were never coming, the rival slips the circlet away into the murk and is gone with it before you understand. The first piece is lost to your own misplaced patience, and you turn back into the cold with empty hands and a hard lesson.

You accept the rival's suddenly offered truce at the dais, and the cold water goes very still and watchful between you. The rival's gloved hands are held open, the circlet drifting just out of your reach, and the dark closes slowly in around the both of you. You wait warily for the promised terms, and you press on.

The corridor floods completely to its ceiling ahead, and the surging current fights you for every hard-won meter. The oppressive cold presses steadily inward from every darkened side. The relentless current tugs insistently at your paying descent line. The way pinches shut against you at last, and you are turned back from it, the recovery set back to another dive.

The passage opens into a vast submerged gallery, where drowned kings watch your slow passage through the green. Somewhere far overhead the entire sunken court shifts and settles, a low groan travelling slowly through the surrounding flooded stone. There is nothing to do now but continue, so you gather your nerve and kick onward into the waiting dark.

The passage constricts to a silt-choked crawl. It closes around your shoulders like a slow, gritty fist. You check your dwindling gauge entirely by feel, carefully counting the thin margin remaining before the turning tide betrays you. The oppressive cold presses steadily inward from every darkened side. You draw one slow, deliberate breath and push on, deeper into the flooded court and its patient cold.

Old wards throb insistently through the water along this stretch, the taste of iron sitting thick in your throat. Fine silt lifts in slow, hanging clouds wherever you so much as touch, drifting through the water like smoke that never clears. The oppressive cold presses steadily inward from every darkened side. The route bends steadily further downward, and you follow it without once allowing yourself to hesitate now.

A submerged gallery unfolds around you, its drowned stone statues staring silently upward through the greenish murk. Fine silt lifts in slow, hanging clouds wherever you so much as touch, drifting through the water like smoke that never clears. The way pinches shut against you at last, and you are turned back from it, the recovery set back to another dive.

The way narrows to a silt-choked crawl. Every careful movement lifts a blinding, suffocating cloud of murk. The heavy green darkness swallows your light barely a body-length ahead, and you navigate by memory and the drifting current alone. There is nothing to do now but continue, so you gather your nerve and kick onward into the waiting dark.

The ancient wards hum steadily louder here, and the surrounding water turns metallic and cold against your tongue. Your narrow lamp carves a thin, wavering cone through the suspended silt, and beyond it the drowned court keeps its patient secrets. You draw one slow, deliberate breath and push on, deeper into the flooded court and its patient cold.

The route becomes a drowned stair, descending endlessly into deeper cold. Your line unspools into the black far below. Penetrating cold works relentlessly into your wrists and jaw, and you flex your numbing fingers to keep them answering your intentions. Nothing whatsoever moves in the heavy green darkness ahead of you. The route bends steadily further downward, and you follow it without once allowing yourself to hesitate now.

Silver bubbles trail upward from a nearby fissure, betraying the rival somewhere dangerously close in the enveloping dark. Somewhere far overhead the entire sunken court shifts and settles, a low groan travelling slowly through the surrounding flooded stone. The oppressive cold presses steadily inward from every darkened side. The relentless current tugs insistently at your paying descent line. You steady yourself, fix the uncertain way ahead firmly in your mind, and press deeper into the drowned court.

There is no passage through here at all, and you retreat into the cold, the regalia still waiting somewhere far below.

The tide surges without any warning and shoves you bodily off your handholds, against the cold, unyielding stone. Somewhere far overhead the entire sunken court shifts and settles, a low groan travelling slowly through the surrounding flooded stone. The oppressive cold presses steadily inward from every darkened side. The relentless current tugs insistently at your paying descent line. You steady yourself, fix the uncertain way ahead firmly in your mind, and press deeper into the drowned court.

Ahead the whole hall has flooded to the ceiling, and you claw forward against a current that shoves relentlessly back. Your laboured breathing echoes strangely through the surrounding drowned silence. The relentless current tugs insistently at your paying descent line. There is nothing to do now but continue, so you gather your nerve and kick onward into the waiting dark.

A ruined stair drops away beneath you into deeper blackness. Your descent line pays steadily out behind you as you go. The heavy green darkness swallows your light barely a body-length ahead, and you navigate by memory and the drifting current alone. The drowned court simply refuses you here, and you haul yourself back the way you came, the dive set back once more.
